planting.note: Direct sow in fall and allow natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seeds for 60 days in damp paper towels in the refrigerator before spring sowing.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this prolific self-seeder quickly naturalizes to form colonies of heart-shaped leaves and violet-blue spring flowers. Both the flowers and young leaves are edible and nutritious — the flowers make beautiful garnishes and violet leaf tea has long been used traditionally. After the showy spring flowers, the plant produces inconspicuous cleistogamous flowers near the soil all summer that set abundant seed; do not confuse these seed capsules with aphid infestations. The plant spreads generously by seed and rhizome — ideal for a naturalized meadow or wild shade garden but potentially aggressive in small formal beds.

planting.note: Plant crown divisions in early spring, keeping the fibrous root ball intact and the crown at soil level; constant moisture is critical during establishment.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this stately native fern is one of the few that thrives in saturated conditions around pond margins, rain gardens, and boggy areas while also adapting to ordinary woodland garden soil. The erect, fertile fronds with their distinctive cinnamon-colored spore cases emerge in the center of the vase-shaped plant in spring and are the source of the common name. Divide the large, fibrous rootball in early spring every 5–7 years; each division should include multiple crowns and a generous portion of roots. The plant is deer resistant and largely pest-free.

planting.note: Plant crowns at soil level in early spring in consistently moist soil; ideal near ponds or streams where roots can access reliable moisture.

directions: Plant in wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the royal fern has a naturally wide distribution across bogs, stream margins, and wet woodlands and is among the largest and most impressive of hardy ferns. The broadly compound fronds have a distinctive open, airy appearance unlike the typical fern, and the fertile frond tips turn rusty brown with spores in summer. Provide consistently wet to saturated soil and avoid any drought stress, particularly in the first two years; mulch heavily with leaf mold to retain moisture. Divide the massive, fibrous root mass in early spring every 7–10 years.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring before bud break; plant two or more for cross-pollination, as single trees produce little fruit.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this adaptable native shrub-tree thrives on poor, dry soils and slopes where improved fruit trees fail, and its thicket-forming habit makes it ideal for erosion control and wildlife habitat plantings. The fragrant white flowers bloom in early spring before the leaves emerge, providing important early pollinator forage. The small red to yellow plums ripen in late summer and are excellent for jams, jellies, wine, and fresh eating; tannin content decreases when fully ripe. American plum spreads by root suckers to form thickets — remove suckers regularly if a tree form is desired, or allow colonization in a naturalized area.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in early spring; this species tolerates a wider range of soil moisture than Amelanchier canadensis and can be grown as a single-trunk tree.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; unlike the colonial serviceberry (A. canadensis), this species develops a single trunk to form a graceful multi-season small tree with spectacular clouds of white flowers in early spring, blueberry-like fruit in June, and brilliant orange-red fall foliage. The sweet berries ripen all at once in early summer and are excellent fresh, dried like raisins, or used in pies and jams; birds compete fiercely for the crop. Prune to remove crossing branches in late winter; do not top the tree. Cedar-apple rust and fire blight are potential problems in humid climates — ensure good air circulation around the canopy.
